questions.question: Explain equal allocation and proportional allocation strategies for frames. Give one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Equal allocation gives same frames to all processes; proportional allocates based on process size or priority
Advantage of equal: simple to implement; Disadvantage: does not consider actual needs
Advantage of proportional: adapts to process size; Disadvantage: may still not consider actual working set

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain why it occurs and how frame allocation can help prevent it.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive page faulting causing poor performance
Occurs when working set exceeds allocated frames
Proper frame allocation maintains working set in memory

questions.question: Differentiate between global replacement and local replacement. Which one generally gives better throughput and why?
keyPoints: Global: process can replace any frame in system; Local: process only from its own frames
Global allows dynamic redistribution
Global generally better throughput as frames go where most needed

questions.question: Explain the working set model for frame allocation. How does it help in determining the number of frames to allocate?
keyPoints: Working set is set of pages actively used during time window Δ
Allocate enough frames to hold working set
If allocated frames < working set size, thrashing occurs

questions.question: What is page fault frequency (PFF) approach to frame allocation? How does the system respond to high and low fault rates?
keyPoints: PFF monitors page fault rate over time
If fault rate > upper threshold: allocate more frames
If fault rate < lower threshold: take away frames

questions.question: Consider a system with 100 frames and 4 processes of sizes: P1=10KB, P2=20KB, P3=30KB, P4=40KB. Calculate the number of frames each process gets using proportional allocation.
keyPoints: Total size = 100KB
P1 gets (10/100)×100 = 10 frames
P2 gets (20/100)×100 = 20 frames
P3 gets 30 frames, P4 gets 40 frames

questions.question: Explain the relationship between degree of multiprogramming and frame allocation per process.
keyPoints: More processes share limited frames
Average frames per process decreases with higher multiprogramming
Can lead to increased page faults and thrashing

questions.question: Discuss the various frame allocation strategies in detail. Compare fixed allocation versus variable allocation and explain which approach is more effective in practice.
keyPoints: Fixed allocation: equal and proportional - frames do not change
Variable allocation: number changes during execution - adaptive
Variable with global replacement most flexible and effective in practice
Must discuss tradeoffs between complexity and performance

questions.question: Explain the working set model and page fault frequency approaches to frame allocation. How do these methods help in preventing thrashing?
keyPoints: Working set: tracks pages used in time window Δ
Allocate frames to contain working set
PFF: monitor fault rate, adjust frames based on thresholds
Both aim to keep sufficient frames for active pages
Prevents thrashing by ensuring working set fits in allocated frames

questions.question: A computer system has 256 frames of physical memory. Currently, 4 processes are running with the following characteristics: P1 (priority 3, size 4KB), P2 (priority 2, size 8KB), P3 (priority 1, size 16KB), P4 (priority 4, size 12KB). Calculate frame allocation using: (a) Equal allocation (b) Proportional allocation by size (c) Proportional allocation by priority. Show your calculations.
keyPoints: Equal: 256/4 = 64 frames each
Proportional by size: total=40KB, P1=(4/40)×256=25.6≈26, P2=51, P3=102, P4=77
Proportional by priority: total=10, P1=(3/10)×256=76.8≈77, P2=51, P3=26, P4=102

questions.question: Explain the difference between local and global page replacement. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each in terms of system performance and memory utilization.
keyPoints: Local: replacement only from own frames; Global: from all frames
Local: predictable per-process behavior, but may waste frames
Global: better utilization, but can affect other processes
Global generally better throughput but requires careful balance
